data.title: Viper Energy: Fast-Growing And Now High-Yield
data.description:
Viper Energy (VNOM) has long been a fast-growing subsidiary of Diamondback Energy (FANG). The latest dropdown from Diamondback has kept that trend going as noted in the last article. This article will take that dropdown and combine it with more information to see where we are heading. As that article also noted, Viper has been buying overriding royalty interests from third parties as well. But Viper is a good deal picker than is the case for many royalty companies in that management tries to buy acreage that will be developed. Even though the company often pays more for its acquisitions, those acquisitions produce results much sooner.

Typical royalty companies receive a low overriding royalty rate over a large amount of acreage that has good and bad locations (hopefully). These companies then wait for the day any of that acreage gets developed. To me, this is more of a lottery ticket approach.

The advantage of considering something like Viper Energy is that the operators of the acreage chosen often have lower costs. Therefore, during times like fiscal year 2020, activity continued on Viper acreage even though oil prices briefly went negative. Oftentimes, both the operators and the acreage have cost advantages that manifest in much better performance during industry downturns.

Viper Energy is still a royalty company that does not control the pace of development of its acreage. But management has a good record of finding deals (for a reasonable price) that result in steady development now instead of maybe later. As a result, the fast growth of production is likely to continue for some time to come.

Stock Price

Since the last article, the stock price has declined considerably because very few saw the effects of the threat of tariffs or a trade war. Many of us really thought this was a decent year ahead at the time. Instead, growth projections have turned negative, and consumer sentiment is down as well.

The stock price has adjusted to the "new outlook" for this industry and likely for the country as well. However, on the assumption that a constructive and predictable structure will replace what is going on now (along with better communication), this stock is a bargain with a historically high yield that has inched into high-yield territory.

Any royalty company should be looked at as a variable distribution entity. More than once, that yield got high before the yield dropped due to lower commodity prices. That could easily happen this time as well.

However, the time to begin to do your research on a company like this is when the industry downturn begins because a company like this should be considered when the outlook is poor. If you believe in free markets, then the cure for low prices is low prices. That means the current situation is likely to set the stage for another 2022 when commodity prices soared and distributions from entities like this one reached previously unimaginable heights.

Protecting the stock price downside is the steady stream of acquisitions that result in relatively fast-growing production over time. Also protecting the downside is the picky selection of investment grade operators and acreage locations that have a cost advantage during downturns. Back in fiscal year 2020, the activity would appear to have been a much superior performance compared to a lot of the industry (when talking about royalty competition).

Leadership Transition

The same leadership transition that is taking place at Diamondback Energy will occur here. Travis Stice, CEO, will be replaced by Kaes Van't Hof (he is currently the president).

This is expected to be a smooth transition. It will likely be a while before the new CEO is completely "on his own". Therefore, it will take some time for him to establish his own long-term record with the company. Nonetheless, for the time being, I would not expect much of a change in the company's strategies.

This transition also increases the chances that the company will likely be sold in the future (or spun off as an independent royalty company). Mr. Stice left some very big shoes to fill as not many have the long-term record he has. The company is also far larger.

Still, it is hard to go from one excellent CEO to another. There just are not that many top performers in any industry. So, this issue will need a bit more monitoring in the near future (at least).

Summary Of Operations

The latest map of operations is shown below, along with some insights into fiscal year 2025.

This is a preliminary outlook of the situation. Since this entity reports a good deal later than many companies I follow, I would expect some sort of update or adjustment that takes into account the political turmoil. That may or may not include some volume adjustments. But any commentary on the industry outlook as a result of what is happening would be helpful for looking at the second quarter or even planning an investment strategy. I would also expect more either confirmations or adjustments as this fiscal year goes on.

The Diamondback organization is fiscally conservative. Therefore, finances are not likely to be an issue in any downturn.

However, this is an excellent way to play the long-term growth potential of the Permian because royalty companies do not have well expenses or exploration dry holes. As a result, the expenses are very low, and the margins are extremely wide. A company like this one with growing production can navigate a period of uncertainty much better than the upstream companies or even other royalty competition.

Summary

Viper Energy is probably one of the best managed royalty companies in the business. During times of uncertainty, a royalty company like this one will often outperform expectations. We could still be in for some rough times due to all the present uncertainty and the effects that a lot of worries could produce.

Therefore, even though this is a strong buy consideration right now, I view the current stock market consideration as an old-fashioned panic. It is best not to stand in front of a runaway truck like Mr. Market. But panics often pass pretty quickly and then value will assert itself rather than the current "throw them all out" attitude that seems to dominate the market.

There are a few ways to ride out the current market panic. But it is also time to get that shopping list ready to go for when you feel comfortable buying bargains.

Risks

This article was written with the idea that the current tariff threat and trade war threats are bargaining positions that will be replaced with something that is positive, well communicated, and permanent before too much damage is done. There can be no assurance that will happen.

Royalty companies do not control the pace of development. Therefore, the growth record established here is not under the control of management.

Royalty companies have less operational expenses than upstream companies, and they have a higher claim to the revenue generated (they get paid "first"). Therefore, during any cyclical downturn, the margins are usually wider for royalty companies than they are for upstream companies.

Nonetheless, a severe and sustained commodity price downturn can materially change the company outlook. Future commodity prices are usually volatile and have very low visibility as has been demonstrated this fiscal year.

The loss of key personnel could prove to be a material setback.

data.title: Microsoft's Cloud Fortress Amid Trump's Tariff Storm
data.description:
In early April 2025, U.S. President Donald Trump enacted sweeping new import tariffs, dubbing April 2 “Liberation Day” as he sought to “free” America from reliance on foreign goods. The policy imposes a baseline 10% tariff on all imports, with much steeper rates on key trade partners. These aggressive tariffs sent shockwaves through global markets and hit hardware-centric tech firms hardest, given their heavy dependence on Asian supply chains.

Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), however, appears relatively insulated. Unlike smartphone or electronics vendors, Microsoft’s revenue is driven mainly by cloud services, software licensing, and subscriptions rather than physical products. In its fiscal 2024, Microsoft generated over $182 billion (roughly three-quarters of total revenue) from its Productivity software and Cloud divisions, while only about one-quarter came from the more hardware-linked “More Personal Computing” segment. This means the bulk of Microsoft’s business – from Azure cloud contracts to Office 365 subscriptions – isn’t directly subject to import taxes on goods.

Nonetheless, Microsoft isn’t completely immune. Potential reciprocal measures by the European Union, China, and other regions loom as risks. If foreign governments retaliate with tariffs on U.S. tech products or impose digital trade barriers, even Microsoft’s cloud-and-software empire could feel some strain. The following analysis delves into how these “Liberation Day” tariffs might impact Microsoft’s operations and outlook – from worst-case hits to its device sales and supply chain, to the ways its software-centric model and strong finances could buffer the storm.

Tariff Mechanics & Microsoft’s Portfolio

Wide-ranging U.S. tariffs primarily target physical supply chains in industries like technology hardware, whereas software and cloud “services” remain exempt for now.

Trump’s “Liberation Day” tariff package has a broad reach, effectively raising the cost of most imported hardware and components. For Microsoft, the most immediate exposure is in its hardware portfolio: Surface laptops and tablets, Xbox gaming consoles, PC accessories, and other devices that Microsoft designs but typically manufactures via contract partners in Asia. These products now potentially incur an extra 10%–50%+ in import taxes when shipped into the U.S., those the final number seems to be changing daily based on one person's whims. In practical terms, Microsoft faces a choice: absorb these higher costs (squeezing hardware profit margins), or pass them on via price hikes (risking lower sales). For instance, a Surface laptop assembled in China might suddenly cost Microsoft 50-125% more in tariffs, given China’s steep levy. A best-case scenario is that the company and its suppliers execute workarounds – e.g. rerouting production to countries with lower tariffs (some reports suggest shifts to Southeast Asia were explored) or using any exempt components to offset costs (indeed, the U.S. tariffs notably exempt semiconductors and other critical electronics to avoid hampering tech firms). Under this best-case outcome, Microsoft’s device prices might only rise modestly (or margins dip slightly), with minimal impact on overall revenue.

In a worst-case scenario, however, tariffs could expand or persist long enough that Microsoft’s hardware sales shrink noticeably. The Xbox console business, for example, is very price-sensitive – a sharp price increase could dent unit sales, especially if rivals (Sony’s PlayStation, etc.) find ways to mitigate their own tariff costs. Similarly, higher PC and tablet prices industry-wide may delay upgrade cycles, indirectly softening demand for Windows licenses. Beyond costs, there’s the specter of tariffs expanding into the software and intellectual property realm, though they are not currently applied there. While import tariffs technically apply only to goods, a protracted trade war could spur less direct barriers – such as requirements that foreign software be subject to special taxes or approval processes (akin to a tariff on digital IP). Were such measures implemented abroad (for instance, China could impose fees on U.S. software licenses, or the EU could pursue a digital services tax in retaliation), Microsoft’s high-margin software business would be exposed. This remains speculative, but it underscores how a trade conflict can broaden beyond physical goods.

Global Reach & Market Presence

Microsoft is a truly global enterprise, with sales spread across every major market – a fact that cuts both ways amid trade tensions. On one hand, geographic diversification means the company isn’t overly beholden to any single region’s economic climate; on the other hand, nearly half of Microsoft’s revenue comes from outside the United States, meaning foreign tariffs or barriers can directly hit roughly 50% of its business.

Crucially, China – often a focal point in trade disputes – accounts for only a small sliver of Microsoft’s revenue. As of 2024, Microsoft’s president Brad Smith noted that China represents roughly 1.5% of total sales. This means that even in an extreme case where China’s market became completely inaccessible due to tariffs or tech restrictions, the direct revenue loss to Microsoft would be limited.

Europe, on the other hand, is a much larger market for Microsoft – a substantial portion of that ~$120 billion “Non-U.S.” revenue comes from European countries. Thus, Europe’s response to Trump’s tariffs is of major importance. So far, U.S. allies like the EU, UK, Japan, and others have voiced disappointment and warned of possible retaliation, but are treading carefully. The EU has historically been willing to enforce its own trade defenses; if the U.S. is taxing European steel, autos, and tech hardware, Brussels could reciprocate with tariffs on U.S. goods.

In 2018’s trade skirmishes, the EU targeted iconic American exports (e.g. bourbon, motorcycles) – this time, large U.S. tech firms could be on the list. A conceivable retaliatory step might be the long-discussed EU digital services tax, which would skim a percentage of revenue from U.S. tech giants operating in Europe. From Microsoft’s perspective, such a tax (for example, a 3% levy on online services revenue in EU countries) would modestly reduce profitability in that region, effectively acting like a tariff on its software services. Europe could also leverage regulation in lieu of tariffs – for instance, enforcing data sovereignty rules that make it harder for U.S. cloud firms to move data freely, or advancing antitrust actions that constrain U.S. companies’ business practices.

Other markets like India, Australia, Japan, and Latin America are also part of Microsoft’s global footprint. Japan is directly hit by the U.S. tariffs (Japanese goods face ~24% U.S. import tariffs under the new plan), which could prompt Japan to exempt or favor domestic suppliers over U.S. software in return (though Japan’s initial response has been “extremely disappointed” but measured). Countries such as India could impose their own “tech self-reliance” policies, accelerating use of local software alternatives as a hedge against geopolitical uncertainty. Microsoft’s strong global presence means it must navigate a patchwork of responses.

Why Microsoft Might Be More Protected

Despite these external risks, Microsoft enjoys several natural defenses that make it more protected than many tech peers in a trade war scenario:

  • Business Mix Skews Toward Software & Cloud: The majority of Microsoft’s revenue comes from software licenses (e.g. Windows, Office), cloud services (Azure, Dynamics 365), and subscriptions (Microsoft 365, GitHub, etc.) which are delivered digitally. Importantly, services are not directly subject to tariffs – the new U.S. duties apply to physical goods, not to cross-border services. This means Microsoft can continue selling an Azure subscription or Office license to a U.S. customer without any tariff cost (whereas a company selling physical network routers from China would incur the import tax). Even internationally, many of Microsoft’s offerings are software downloads or cloud-based, which generally aren’t taxed like imported commodities.
  • Limited Reliance on Physical Manufacturing: Microsoft does produce hardware (Xbox, Surface, accessories), but unlike, say, Apple (AAPL)– which relies on huge volumes of devices for its revenue – Microsoft’s hardware is a small proportion of total sales (estimated well under 10% of revenue). The company has flexibility to de-emphasize or reprioritize products if needed. In fact, Microsoft’s latest earnings indicated that even as device revenue declined, overall gross margins still rose thanks to a sales mix shift to higher-margin businesses (cloud/software). This highlights that Microsoft can weather a slump in hardware by leaning on its more profitable segments. It also has the option to redesign supply chains for key devices: for example, components could be dual-sourced from multiple countries, and final assembly could be moved to tariff-exempt regions like Mexico or other allies (Microsoft’s manufacturing is outsourced, giving it some agility to negotiate with manufacturing partners about location). During the 2019 tariff threats, it was reported (though later downplayed) that Microsoft explored moving Xbox and Surface production out of China to mitigate potential tariffs. Even if not fully executed then, those contingency plans can be dusted off now.
  • Resilient Customer Base with Subscription Stickiness: A large portion of Microsoft’s revenue is recurring, coming from enterprise agreements and subscriptions that are not easily canceled. Corporate IT departments are unlikely to rip out Microsoft software due to a tariff – the switching costs and productivity loss would far outweigh a slight price increase due to trade policies. Similarly, Office 365 subscribers or Azure clients are fairly “sticky.” This subscription model provides stability: even if new sales in a region slow down due to economic jitters, Microsoft’s existing contracts keep generating revenue.
  • Financial Firepower and Operational Flexibility: Microsoft enters this trade conflict from a position of extraordinary financial strength. It has a fortress balance sheet, with over $75 billion in cash and short-term investments on hand and minimal net debt. Its annual operating cash flow exceeds $90 billion, yielding robust free cash flow of around $60+ billion per year. This gives Microsoft ample ability to absorb cost increases or temporarily sacrifice margin to maintain market share.

Potential Points of Vulnerability

No company of Microsoft’s size is completely shielded in a global trade war. We now turn to the specific vulnerabilities and scenarios where Microsoft could feel real pain from the tariffs and ensuing reactions:

  • Supply Chain and Data Center Costs: Microsoft operates a vast global cloud infrastructure, with over 200 data centers in dozens of countries powering Azure and other services. Building and refreshing these data centers involves massive procurement of servers, networking gear, power systems, and construction materials. Many of these capital goods have international supply chains. Higher tariffs on imported tech hardware – even components like racks, cooling systems, or networking switches – could raise Microsoft’s cost to expand and upgrade its cloud. For instance, if certain high-end servers are assembled in Taiwan (facing 32% U.S. import tariff) and Microsoft needs them for U.S. data centers, that’s a direct cost increase. Even if semiconductors are tariff-exempt, items like power supplies or memory modules might not be.
  • Regulatory and Consumer Backlash Abroad: Trade wars often fuel nationalist sentiment. Microsoft, as a prominent American tech company, could encounter softer forms of retaliation in key foreign markets such as public opinion or regulatory scrutiny. For example, in China, there is an ongoing push for self-sufficiency in technology. High-profile U.S. trade aggression can strengthen Chinese regulators’ resolve to favor domestic tech. We might see Chinese government agencies accelerate plans to replace Windows with indigenous operating systems on official computers, or enterprises might be subtly encouraged to opt for local cloud providers (Alibaba (BABA), Huawei Cloud) over Azure. Even if not an official policy, a rise in anti-U.S. tech sentiment can create headwinds for Microsoft’s sales teams in those countries. Europe might not boycott U.S. products, but regulators there are already very active in regulating Big Tech (antitrust cases, privacy fines, etc.). A politically charged environment could make Microsoft a convenient target – for instance, if the EU feels economic pressure from U.S. tariffs, they might double down on enforcing the Digital Markets Act and force changes to Microsoft’s business practices in Windows/Office bundling or cloud interoperability, which could level the playing field for European competitors.

Valuation & Financials

From a valuation and financial standpoint, Microsoft enters this tariff period on solid footing. Wall Street’s confidence in Microsoft’s fundamentals is reflected in its premium valuation multiples. As of April 2025, MSFT trades around 28–30× forward earnings and about 19× EV/EBITDA – notably higher than the S&P 500 average. This premium is underpinned by Microsoft’s strong growth (analysts expect low to mid-teens EPS growth) and its defensive qualities.

A look at free cash flow [FCF] and returns on capital is instructive. In FY24, Microsoft generated roughly $65–70 billion in free cash flow, and it returned about $38 billion to shareholders via buybacks and dividends over that year. The company’s ability to consistently turn a high percentage of its earnings into free cash (thanks to software’s low capital requirements) gives it a war chest to handle adversity. Free cash flow yield stands around 3% (meaning investors get $3 of FCF for every $100 invested in stock, in line with 10-year Treasury yields). This isn’t a high yield, but it’s considered acceptable given the reliability and growth of that cash flow.

Balance sheet strength is exemplary: as noted, Microsoft has over $75 billion in cash and short-term investments vs. about $50 billion in total debt, leaving a net cash position (unlike many big peers that carry net debt). This net cash is like an insurance policy – it could be used for strategic acquisitions (taking advantage of weaker competitors in turbulent times) or simply to buffer dividends/buybacks if operating cash flow ever stumbled. Credit agencies rate Microsoft debt AAA, a sign of rock-solid finances.

To gauge how tariffs might affect Microsoft’s valuation, consider a basic DCF or earnings multiple approach. If we assume, say, a worst-case $2–3 billion hit to annual net income from various tariff and retaliation effects (a rough “few percentage points” of profit), that might reduce EPS by ~$0.25–0.30. Before tariffs, FY2025 EPS was estimated around $13.40; a minor hit could bring that to ~$13.10. At a ~29× multiple, that’s roughly $8–$9 off the stock’s theoretical value.

It’s also useful to compare Microsoft’s valuation with peers amid the tariffs: Apple, for instance, is in the crossfire of device tariffs and trades around 27× earnings; Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG), less hardware-exposed, trades near 25×; Amazon, with heavy import costs for its retail side, is more complicated but its AWS cloud is tariff-immune. Microsoft’s premium to most peers signals that the market views it as one of the best positioned to handle the trade war.

Additional Considerations

Historical Perspective: Microsoft is no stranger to navigating turbulent regulatory and trade environments. A look back at the late 2010s trade war between the U.S. and China shows that Microsoft’s core business proved resilient. While companies like Apple and Nvidia saw significant volatility from tariff announcements, Microsoft was often cited as comparatively safer – indeed, during a 2019 downturn, Microsoft briefly became the world’s most valuable company as investors sought stability.

Additionally, Microsoft has weathered strict regulatory regimes (such as antitrust battles in the late 1990s/early 2000s in the U.S. and EU) and still emerged as a dominant, profitable firm. Those experiences honed Microsoft’s ability to adapt – for instance, dealing with the EU’s requirement to offer a browser choice in Windows, or opening up APIs to third parties. Compared to those existential antitrust threats, a trade tariff issue seems manageable. Microsoft’s longevity (it has been through recessions, wars, and currency crises in its 40+ year history) suggests that temporary trade policy shifts won’t derail its strategic trajectory. One caveat: the global economy is more intertwined now, and Microsoft is far larger, so macro disruptions can have bigger absolute effects even if the business model is robust. Still, investors can take some comfort in Microsoft’s track record of steady performance amid external storms.

Behind the scenes, Microsoft is surely not sitting idle. Microsoft has a significant policy and government affairs operation. We can expect Microsoft, perhaps through industry groups, to lobby the U.S. administration for exemptions or adjustments relevant to its business. For example, during earlier tariff rounds, tech companies successfully argued for exemptions on certain components (hence semiconductors being exempt now). Microsoft might push for similarly nuanced policies – such as not extending tariffs to software or pushing for a quicker resolution. Likewise, Microsoft can engage with the EU and others to discourage digital retaliation, highlighting how many European companies rely on Azure/Office, so hurting Microsoft could backfire on their own economies. Satya Nadella and President Brad Smith are known to be diplomatically active; Smith in particular frequently testifies to governments and could play a role in finding common ground to avoid worst-case trade outcomes.

Conclusion

In sum, Microsoft appears well-positioned to withstand the April 2025 “Liberation Day” tariffs storm, albeit with a few umbrellas at the ready for possible rain. The company’s strengths – a predominant software/cloud revenue base, diversified global operations, deep financial resources, and a track record of strategic adaptability – provide a strong defense against direct tariff impacts. Unlike hardware-heavy tech giants that face immediate pricing and supply chain turmoil, Microsoft’s core businesses can continue relatively unimpeded by import taxes on goods. Its robust cloud growth and recurring software revenues serve as a ballast, keeping overall performance steady even as the devices segment encounters choppy waters. Indeed, Microsoft’s relative insulation is evident in that it has not had to drastically alter its outlook; the company continues to project solid growth, merely acknowledging some near-term uncertainty in hardware-related areas.

data.title: Buy, Buy, Bad Week. Time To Get Greedy
data.description:
Introduction

This may have been the most fascinating week for me in my career.

While the pandemic and prior election cycles were very interesting, this week topped it all, as we got bombarded by market-moving political headlines almost every single day.

I had calls with insiders almost daily and watched the market break many records, both bullish and bearish ones.

On top of that, I put more than half of my cash to work.

  • I boosted my investment in Old Dominion Freight Line (ODFL) by 65%.
  • My investment in Comfort Systems USA (FIX) was expanded by 55%.
  • I boosted my Rexford Industrial Real Estate (REXR) investment by 28%.

These investments may not come as a surprise, as I have written a number of very bullish articles, including "My Worst Week Ever – And The 4 Dividend Stocks I'm Buying Now."

For now, being a buyer was the right decision, as the market has made a substantial comeback, as the S&P 500 made the third-biggest one-day comeback since the end of the Second World War, as it rose by 9.5% on Wednesday, when the president announced a 90-day tariff pause on all nations except for China.

Interestingly enough, eight of the best ten days occurred over the past 17 years, with three events dominating volatility:

  • The Great Financial Crisis
  • The 2020 pandemic
  • The 2025 trade war (or whatever you want to call it)

Another record we broke was the decline in volatility.

Last week, we had the third-biggest volatility spike since 1990, as the Cboe Volatility Index rose by 109%. This week, we had the single-biggest one-day VIX decline, as the volatility index fell by 36%.

Just like the biggest declines, these declines tend to be followed by outperforming returns. On average, the 20 biggest VIX declines were followed by outperforming total returns on a 1/2/4/5 year basis, as the table above shows.

This makes sense, exaggerated moves (to both sides) usually happen during periods of stock price weakness. Using the data below, half of the best trading days occurred in years with negative total returns.

Steep market declines are often followed by days of relief, which are much more pronounced in bear markets than bull markets. After all, during most bull markets when everything seems to be fine, there's usually not a lot that can happen to cause relief.

That's why it is so important for long-term investors (for traders, it's different) to stay put when things go south, as even missing a few of the best trading days tends to ruin one's long-term performance, as the data below shows.

So, what does this rebound mean?

Is the coast clear?

You're going to hate this answer, but I don't know.

What I do know is that risks are now substantially lower.

For example, the 90-day tariff pause has reduced headline risks, as the environment is now much more predictable. It allows policymakers to reassess the situation and negotiate on tariffs. Companies also have more certainty, as they assess how to operate in various tariff environments.

In fact, it even allows China to re-route goods to the U.S., as it has spent many years building supply chains in nations like Vietnam and Mexico.

However, all things considered, I believe Treasury Secretary Bessent may have been the driving force here, as he likely got Trump to implement the pause so early due to the massive risks that rising bond yields posed for the economy and market stability.

The Wall Street Journal seems to agree with me:

Bessent was flooded with worried calls from Wall Street over the weekend and felt strongly he had to persuade Trump that a pause was needed. It wouldn’t be a capitulation, Bessent argued, because they were going to have so many deals.

He revealed little publicly about why the president and his team waited until Wednesday afternoon to enact it, with Trump saying that he decided on the move Wednesday morning. Bessent said more than 75 countries have reached out seeking a deal to ease tariffs, with Japan “at the front of the queue.” - WSJ (emphasis added)

I think it's a great strategy, as the U.S. is not 'forcing' restoring anymore by implementing tariffs that could do substantial short-term damage, but by the constant fear of new tariffs that are now in the air.

This puts the U.S. in a good spot to work on deals and get companies to move some production to the United States. After all, I always disliked the method of forcing everyone, as there is no way a nation like the U.S. can compete with $1/hr jobs in Vietnam. These jobs are unlikely to move to the U.S.

If we can refrain from further escalation, I believe the situation for economic re-shoring in the U.S. is quite favorable and a confirmation of my longer-term thesis.

Then there's the news of lower inflation.

In March, inflation fell to 2.4%, 20 basis points below expectations.

Although it needs to be seen what the impact of tariffs will be on the April report, we can assume that the massive decline in oil prices will be a favorable factor.

While my 'higher for longer' thesis is still valid, these shorter-term inflation declines add some safety to the market, as it puts the Fed in a better spot to ease rates a bit.

Our expectation is that when push comes to shove, the FOMC will cut the federal funds rate to limit damage to the labor market, even if inflation moves higher in the months ahead. We project the federal funds rate to be 125 bps lower by the end of the year amid a modest rise in the unemployment and economic growth that is near zero for the year. - Wells Fargo

It also helps to lower longer-term bond rates, which is critical, as they have a big impact on financial markets (the lower the risk-free rate, the higher the attractiveness of stocks) and because both corporations and the government can use lower rates to refinance debt.

After peaking at 4.5% before the tariff pause, the 10-year has seen a drop in its yield to 4.3%, which I expect to continue, providing more relief for the market.

Despite all of this, I am not convinced we are about to make it back to all-time highs.

That's a big part of the reason why I remain very selective when it comes to asset allocation.

Buying Weakness The Smart Way

I believe it's crucial to zoom out a bit.

That includes the fact that markets started to weaken before President Trump's 'Liberation Day' press conference, which marked the start of the turmoil we're currently in.

In February, the market started to gain downside momentum, led by cyclical industries like transportation stocks. A big part of this was triggered by a rotation from American stocks to international alternatives, including European equities.

This was not necessarily a recession preparation, but mainly a move where money shifted to areas with a more attractive risk/reward. This was triggered by the belief that President Trump may worsen America's relationship with European partners and potentially impose hefty tariffs. The latter turned out to be the case.

As of March 31, we see that U.S. stocks were trading at a massive valuation premium compared to non-American stocks. With AI stocks losing momentum and the unpredictability of the new president, markets saw this as a good opportunity to rotate, which is exactly what they did.

It also needs to be said that the U.S., in general, had the issue of being tech-heavy, as its ten biggest holdings accounted for 35% of its weighting, with an average P/E ratio of 24.4x.

Given the rise in interest rates, unfavorable valuation differences, and the convergence in growth expectations, the rotation from growth to value stocks started to heat up, as we can see in the chart below, which shows the growth/value ratio and growth estimates for 2025.

So far this year, growth stocks are lagging value stocks by slightly more than 400 basis points, although both growth and value are in negative territory.

Needless to say, value stocks include America's highest-quality dividend stocks, which are represented by ETFs like the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) in the chart below.

Here are its top holdings:

Most of these dividend stocks have a few things in common:

  • Business models with an edge. Especially in this uncertain environment, I only buy companies that bring something special to the table. Companies with "mission-critical" business models.
  • They all have healthy balance sheets. During the pandemic, I bought companies with poor balance sheets as turnaround investments. Now, I don't make exceptions anymore, as the debt market has become a minefield.
  • Most of them have decent valuations.

Using the Russell 2000, we see that value stocks have valuations very close to the long-term median, while growth stocks are trading close to 30x earnings.

Unless we enter a steep recession, I believe these levels offer tremendous opportunities.

While I also like the opportunity that some tech stocks present, I think the best way forward is to stick with value/high-quality dividend stocks, as we're not out of the woods yet.

Needless to say, I'll keep you up to date on my investments, economic developments, and provide more actionable ideas.

Takeaway

This week was a wild ride, one of the most intense of my career.

However, it was also one of the most rewarding, as I deployed more than half my cash into high-conviction stocks like ODFL, FIX, and REXR.

While market swings were wild, it's the kind of chaos that often sets the stage for opportunity—opportunities long-term investors like me need to build wealth.

With headline risks easing a bit, inflation dropping (at least temporarily), and a likely decline in long-term yields, I believe the worst-case scenarios are less likely.

Nonetheless, I'm staying selective, focusing on quality dividend/value plays with strong balance sheets and durable business models.

I don't think we're racing back to all-time highs with the support of 'big tech' just yet, but I do think this is a fertile environment for long-term investors who can keep a cool head and buy strategically.

data.title: Kura Sushi: Fire In Gasoline As Tariff Pause Sparks A Rally
data.description:
Fire in Gasoline

Funny how things turn around.

Kura Sushi (KRUS) had been sliding with the market after the whole ‘Liberation Day’ news and some pretty bleak sentiment. But all it took was a 90-day pause on tariffs and a smoother-than-expected transition quarter and... boom, Kura Sushi stock shot up nearly 30% on Wednesday.

Well, who am I to complain? I had just upgraded the stock to a ‘Buy’ with a $90 price target after watching this growth story lose nearly half its market cap since the start of the year.

It was time to bite this temaki near the support zone! After all, share prices behave very cyclically, and it was obvious that it was time to go shopping.

But of course, things had to get a little messy first.

The White House came out swinging with tariffs on the so-called ‘worst offenders,’ and whatever was left of market optimism pretty much vanished. Inflation expectations jumped, growth expectations dropped, and the market — already bleeding since February — took another hit as the full scope of the measures became clear.

For restaurants, the signal was loud and clear: tighter wallets ahead. Fewer visits, lower average checks, guests were about to get even more selective with their spending.

So who comes out on top in a setup like this?

Easy answer. The spots that give you the most bang for your buck, the famous 'value-for-dollar'. Think steakhouses like Texas Roadhouse (TXRH) and LongHorn from Darden (DRI), both masters at delivering tremendous value.

Even Cracker Barrel (CBRL), with its early-bird specials — basically Texas Roadhouse for the family dining crowd — should keep riding the wave, especially now that the turnaround is starting to stick.

The biggest losers?

In my opinion, concepts like fad fast-casuals with high-price points could lose steam. Of course, fast-food restaurants where you wouldn't get a good value-for-dollar ratio would be decimated again, following the cycle of declining value scores.

Let’s be real, Kura Sushi is a fun and good spot for Japanese food. But it’s not exactly your go-to for everyday value. Price points run a bit higher than what you’d find at your typical casual dining place. It’s almost the same as GEN Korean BBQ (GENK).

A cut in discretionary spending would definitely hurt this growth story. The CEO himself said he saw a tariff-related slowdown in April. It’s probably the wealth effect kicking in. After all, we just saw a sharp market drop and a wave of selling in retirement funds.

But that’s not all.

Don’t believe me? Let’s look at some numbers.

First off, the Trump Tariffs would tack on an extra $300,000 to $400,000 in construction costs per store. That pushes the total cost of opening a new Kura location to somewhere between $2.8 and $2.9 million, a pretty steep jump of 12% to 16% in buildout costs! Consider that they plan to open 14 locations during FY 2025. Assuming stable construction costs throughout the year, they would add at least $4.2 to 5.6 million in full-year Capex to build out the same stores.

But if you zoom in a little more, the hit gets even clearer.

We’re talking about an extra year just to reach the payback period — plus a 10% to 14% drop in the cost-benefit ratio. That’s a big deal for a growth story built on opening new units efficiently.

However, those are still solid numbers compared to other restaurant chains.

Take Cheesecake Factory (CAKE), for example. Their restaurants are massive, and while they pull in nearly $8 million in AUV, the buildout costs average around $4.5 million. That puts their payback period in the 4-to-5-year range, with a cost-benefit ratio that’s about 12% lower than Kura Sushi, even in the worst-case scenario under the tariffs.

Another example is GEN Korean BBQ. They also have a three-year payback period, but demonstrate a lower Cost-Benefit Ratio of 7.74%. And even the heavyweight Texas Roadhouse isn’t immune to this. With a massive $8.5 million per unit cost, they’re looking at a six-year payback and a Cost-Benefit Ratio of just 2.87%.

So yeah, while it wasn’t exactly a blowout, Kura Sushi’s Q2 FY2025 still did its job.

It reaffirmed the growth story.

They held steady on their full-year outlook, including double-digit unit growth and a 20% unit-level margin. Now they just need to get sales momentum back and start spreading those fixed costs around.

But let’s be real — it wasn’t just this reaffirmation that got the stock moving today.

The tariff pause changes a few things right off the bat:

  1. More time to make reciprocal agreements;
  2. It reduces the chances of an economic slowdown in the coming months. Doesn’t take it off the table completely, but it definitely eases the pressure;
  3. Perhaps some dissenting voices within the Trump cycle may be acting to dissuade this policy.

This likely means we get at least one more quarter of somewhat normal consumer spending and stable inflation. That’s great news for Kura! It gives them more breathing room to work things out with suppliers (who already said they’ll take on much of the tariff hit), fine-tune the marketing plan, and keep pushing the development pipeline forward.

The combination of these two factors was like fire on gasoline. The stock was already going up slightly today after the release of Q2 FY 2025 results. But the tariff pause was almost the best of both worlds.

A wave of capital came rushing back into the stock market, and suddenly, names that had been trading well below their peer group and historical averages started catching some well-deserved attention.

Same-Store Sales Drop Scares, But Doesn't Bite

I grew up watching cartoons, and honestly, watching what Kura Sushi went through earlier this year felt like one of those classic beatdowns. To get into the oriental atmosphere, not even Goku would come out of this unscathed.

Blizzards, California wildfires, tariffs, IP programming gaps, cannibalization — you name it. Phew! I’m getting exhausted just listing it all.

By the end of the day, sales continued to climb. They were $64.9 million—up at least 13.2% year-over-year. This was driven by two factors:

  1. Double-digit growth in the number of restaurants year-over-year; and
  2. Outperforming units that have recently opened (Bakersfield, CA is a great example of this).

This double-digit revenue growth for the quarter was partially offset by weaker same-store sales.

They fell 5.3% year-over-year in Q2 FY 2024. This represented a 710 bp slowdown from the previous quarter. Breaking that down a bit, we have an 8.5% year-over-year decline in traffic—accelerating the sequential decline from FY 2024—offset by average check per guest growth of 3.2% year-over-year.

But wait, let me explain why this isn’t as bad as you might think.

First off, as I mentioned, Kura Sushi is expanding its footprint at a seriously fast pace — and I mean very fast. Stores like the ones in Bakersfield or even the ones in the Northwest aren't included in the comparable stores count.

That means their stronger-than-expected performances can only be glimpsed through revenue.

Another factor, as I mentioned earlier, was natural phenomena. Management estimates that weather alone created a drag of around 400 to 500 basis points! If there were no headwinds in this regard (I mean, literally) Kura Sushi would see same-store sales decline by only 1.3% year-over-year to 0.3% year-over-year depending on traffic impacts.

Just look. That wouldn’t have been too bad. Black Box Intelligence showed the industry was basically flat — up 2.5% year-over-year in January and down 2.5% in February, canceling each other out.

The third and final factor is: Management already knew Q2 FY 2025 would be the softest quarter of the year — thanks to the IP gap and some tough comps from last year’s hit 'Peanuts' campaign. I actually flagged this in my last piece on Kura Sushi — they fumbled the timing of their marketing campaign, and now they’re playing catch-up in the second half of FY 2025.

See? They look very angry, but they don't bite.

Now that we've got the dots on the i's and crosses on the t's, let's talk about something that hurt the P&L in Q2: increases in labor costs.

They grew from 180 bp to 34.8% due to the deleveraging of sales. The remedy for this is simple, but not always so quick: increase sales volume.

The deleveraging of sales also hurt SG&A, which also increased in importance relative to revenue. Additionally, Kura Sushi reported a litigation settlement expense of $2.1 million.

All of this was offset by lower Food, Beverage and Packaging costs of 90 bp to 28.7%.

Still, that wasn’t enough to stop the bleeding. EBIT came in negative at $4.6 million.

With a small tailwind from Investment Income, Kura Sushi reported a net loss of $3.8 million — that’s EPS of -$0.31, down $0.22 from the same period last year. Normalized and diluted EPS came in at -$0.19, a $0.14 drop year-over-year.

Not ideal, sure, but considering everything we’ve walked through above, it still lines up with a growth story that’s just hitting a few bumps along the way.

And hey, you’re not the only one who saw an opportunity in the bear market. Kura Sushi deployed $15.3 million into marketable and equity securities. Since these are highly liquid, the company still ended the quarter with $85.2 million in cash and zero long-term debt.

Not bad at all.

But let’s get to the heart of the thesis: pipeline expansion.

Kura’s Conveyor of Growth Keeps Moving

In Q2 FY 2024, Kura Sushi opened three new restaurants: Berkeley, CA; Fort Worth, TX; Paramus, NJ. For Q3 so far they have also opened two restaurants: Scottsdale, AZ and Lynnwood, WA.

It’s worth pointing out that these new openings still don’t fully reflect the ‘50-50 Strategy’ management introduced after identifying cannibalization risks.

Kura still has a heavy footprint in California (21 units), Texas (12), and New Jersey (5). These locations were probably greenlit a while ago.

Remember, the real shift was promised for next year—that’s when we’ll see whether the new game plan kicks in for real.

Things really started to get interesting last year. When they opened in places like Tacoma, WA; Beaverton, OR; and especially Bakersfield, CA, it became clear—they didn't need to chase those big, flashy DMAs to pull strong AUVs.

Even mid-to-low DMA areas were showing strong performance and sky-high AUVs.

In other words, if it weren't for Tacoma's success they would never have doubled down on Washington with Lynnwood!

That basically unlocked a whole new white space for Kura to grow into. That said, they’re now setting their sights on markets like Birmingham, Tulsa, Boise, and Oklahoma City. New additions in Boulder, CO and Salt Lake City, UT are coming soon.

Just to recap, Kura Sushi currently has 75 restaurants. They opened five this year alone and are on track to reach their guidance goal of 14 openings throughout FY 2025.

This brings the restaurant count to 84 locations and at least one more opening throughout FY 2025 (through August of this year).

Another double-digit year-over-year unit growth is practically guaranteed.

FY 2025 Guidance Reiterated

Further proof that a soft Q2 was expected? Look no further than management reaffirming full-year guidance during the last earnings call.

If you don't remember or didn't see it, it's as follows:

  • Total sales are between $275 million and $279 million (estimated growth of 16% year-over-year to 17% year-over-year);
  • Openings of 14 units;
  • Average net capital expenditures per unit of approximately $2.5 million ($35 million Capex for openings in the consolidated year);
  • G&A as a percentage of sales is approximately 13.5% (based on revenue guidance it would be $37.12 million to $37.6 million, growing roughly 10% year-over-year).

My Recommendation Is Still 'Buy'

Kura Sushi continues to be a delicious growth story—with a built-in margin of safety of roughly 40% based on a price target of $90.

And if you were sharp enough to buy during the last Trump Tariff dip at $43? You didn’t just lock in over 30% upside—you walked away with a juicy 52% margin of safety.

With an estimated RPS of around $27.43 (taking into account estimated revenue of $331.95 million for FY 2026), Kura Sushi is trading at a P/S of approximately 1.98x.

Sounds high, right?

But keep in mind, Kura trades at a 5-year average sales multiple of just over 4x.

That’s relatively modest when you compare it to other fast-growing restaurant names like Shake Shack (SHAK), Wingstop (WING), and CAVA (CAVA), which all command significantly higher premiums thanks to their growth profiles.

But we don’t need to dwell on estimates. On a historical basis, Kura Sushi still has much lower multiples than other high-growth restaurant chains:

Back when Kura Sushi was trading near resistance earlier this year, its P/S multiple was actually more stretched than CAVA’s. Since then, as growth cooled, the gap widened.

But here’s the thing—even with CAVA and Wingstop pulling back double digits year-to-date, they still look pricey on both P/E and EV/S metrics.

That’s not the case for my two favorite names in the high-growth restaurant space right now: Kura Sushi and Shake Shack, especially after their recent pullbacks.

That said, I’m reiterating my ‘Buy’ rating on Kura Sushi, with a price target of $90.

data.title: HTD: Don't Let Taxes Distract You From Compounding
data.description:
The John Hancock Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund (HTD) was created for folks whose tax situation may require them to care about the tax treatment of their portfolio income. Yet, dividends are dividends, and this fund offers a steady yield among dependable securities known for regular distributions from essential business models. For that reason, I consider it a reasonable buy for the investor who seeks to navigate a potential bear market through dividend reinvestment.

Historical Results

The benefits of this fund show themselves when we look at the total returns of HTD over the last decade.

While the S&P 500 has come out ahead in the past decade, we see this mostly occurred in the last few years, on the heels of an AI-driven, tech-focus bulled run. During years when there is less excitement in the markets, however, HTD offered a clear advantage through dividend reinvestment and compounding.

This is in spite of the fact that dividends haven't grown much over time, being cyclical if anything. Nevertheless, they've been a steady source of return, even in times of stress, such as the days of the COVID pandemic. Despite the lack of an increasing dividend, it does go to show what is possible with simple reinvestment.

Composition and Strategy

In a mission to provide tax-advantaged dividends to shareholders, HTD primarily invests in U.S. common stocks that are known to be dividend payers.

For this reason, the fund is heavily concentrated on utilities and financials. It's worth noting, however, that not everything fits into this neat box of dividend stocks.

Above, a significant part of this sector allocation is realized through corporate bonds, not only common stocks. Some of these issues span into the next century, commanding a very high yield. If the income is attractive, it seems that HTD's manager doesn't let the potential tax treatment get in the way. As of their most recent statement of holdings, these bonds made up 31% of the portfolio. Some preferred stock also finds its way into the portfolio.

The fund also utilizes some leverage, often up to a third of their total assets. Their liquidity agreement with State Street Bank and Trust Company provides them a line of credit, securities lending, and reverse repurchase agreements to finance these investments. To hedge some of this interest rate risk on the financing, they also own some swap contracts. In the event of a market crash, they may have to liquidate some of their holdings as collateral to repay these loans.

With the interest expense from their financing making up most of the expenses in this fund, the management's cut is actually a fee of 1.13%.

In the grand scheme, this is a portfolio weighted on American utilities and financials. Mostly in stocks, it generally has some long-term growth opportunities, but it also pursues high-yielding bonds from some of these same issuers where possible. With a bit of leverage, this helps to juice up its yield.

Future Outlook

One of the good things about this fund is that its holdings weren't trading at huge multiples like many other stock funds, particularly those with tech exposure. While many were worried about a stock market bubble, a portfolio primarily of utilities and financials is largely an interest rate game. As rates increased starting in 2022, valuations were pushed down.

The share price started to recover in 2024, likely due to expectations of an eventual rate cut, and it picked up that September once the Fed's first cut after its hike regime was announced. With a selloff in the market in response to Trump's tariffs, HTD is down over 10% from its recent highs and trades near an 8% yield.

If a bear market lays ahead, HTD shows a portfolio of securities that weren't already overvalued and that can be reinvested at a good rate of return. As rate cuts have already been announced, albeit more slowly than they were hiked, over time this should support capital gains.

For the utilities in the portfolio, which are often fairly leveraged themselves, rate cuts should help them improve their interest expense and increase free cash flow to support the common stocks' dividend growth as well.

Risks

I expect most of the risk in the portfolio to be among the financials. In covering other investments, I've at times talked about the state of commercial real estate, which saw a surge of mortgages in 2021, following COVID stimulus and rate cuts to near-zero. Many of those loans have experienced delinquencies and needed to be restructured, especially last year, as the assets have been troubled by higher operating costs from inflation and lower-than-expected tenant occupancy.

A recent piece, considering the impact of tariffs, by CBRE observes:

Industrial and retail leasing demand likely will see the most direct impacts as consumer spending decreases and the flow of goods shifts. Other property types, such as multifamily, office and hotels, would experience secondary effects of slower growth, which would include less demand and new supply as construction costs rise.

Additionally, leasing decisions by commercial tenants can be delayed when they are considering what will need to be done in the U.S. (if the tariffs stick or if Trump is using them to make a deal). For the borrowers who need to rent to repay those mortgages, it's a tough position, and the lenders are left in a pickle if these loans on their balance sheet can't be repaid.

Even if it doesn't lead to a 2008-level crisis, it can depress earnings and reduce the dividend income provided by the shares.

Even a major bank like Wells Fargo (WFC) had to cut its dividend in response to COVID, and its quarterly distribution hasn't fully recovered. Newer macroeconomic setbacks that further weaken loans, which are already being tested, stand out to me as the clearest, most likely risk for such a portfolio.

These risks not only could cause dividend cuts by the issuers; an associated selloff could trigger margin calls on HTD's leverage and force them to lock in losses in order to maintain liquidity.

Conclusion

While the John Hancock Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund isn't the most exciting dividend fund or a portfolio with enormous upside ahead, it does have the right pieces for a simple response to the looming threat of a bear market that may last years ahead: reinvesting dividends. Drawing on businesses that provide an essential role in the economy, HTD should be able to collect and distribute its investment income in most scenarios, with additional upside provided by not only capital gains on a rate but improvements in the balance sheets of their holdings.

For those reasons, I consider HTD to be a decent 'Buy.'

data.title: Palantir: Upgrading To Buy, Tariffs Would Be A Win-Win (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I've covered Palantir Technologies (PLTR) several times in the past few months with a consistently bearish tone highlighting extreme valuation risks with the stock. While valuation risks certainly remain, the stock has re-entered orbit with the recent market selloff, warranting a reevaluation of the investment case.

As a brief overview, Palantir sells enterprise software used in a variety of end markets. For years, the company was primarily a government defense contractor offering a mission-critical software known as Gotham. Gotham, similar to Palantir's other products Foundry and Artificial Intelligence Platform ("AIP"), specializes in connecting disparate data sources into one common backend feed and producing a simplified interface and better analytics capabilities. This feeds into Palantir's "Ontology" framework, which is a methodology of representing disparate data in a common format. It essentially allows operators to view a digital model of their organization in a seamless interface. Adding AIP and Ontology together gives operators an agent to use within Ontology, which has clear value-add for enterprises. The ROI is worth the spend and enterprises have been flooding to Palantir Bootcamps.

Given this context, the stock has been in a parabolic upswing for the past year. The Bootcamp strategy has been extremely successful to date and I see no reason for deceleration. The company's US enterprise segment has performed exceptionally well in 2024 and will continue this trend in 2025.

From the Q4 '24 reports, Palantir reported the following highlights:

  • U.S. revenue up 52% YoY and 12% QoQ to $558 million
    • U.S. commercial revenue grew 64% YoY and 20% QoQ to $214 million
    • U.S. government revenue grew 45% YoY and 7% QoQ to $343 million
  • Revenue grew 36% YoY and 14% QoQ to $828 million
  • Closed 129 deals of at least $1 million, 58 deals of at least $5 million, and 32 deals of at least $10 million
  • Closed a record-setting $803 million of U.S. commercial total contract value (“TCV”), up 134% YoY and 170% QoQ
  • U.S. commercial remaining deal value (“RDV”) of $1.79 billion, up 99% YoY and 47% QoQ
  • Customer count grew 43% YoY and 13% QoQ

While these results certainly highlight the company's traction, the issue comes when you look at the stock's market cap. For a company with $828m in quarterly revenue, even if it's growing at 40% annually, $180 billion is far too steep a price to pay. At this price, a 2% upward move in the stock represents a market cap increase of $3.6b, eclipsing full year 2024 revenue by nearly a billion dollars. The math does not add up.

Yet, with as sharp a selloff as we've seen the past few weeks, the stock valuation is in a better place than it was at the beginning of the year. Palantir actually stands to benefit from a higher tariff environment, which I'll make the case for below. Further, the ongoing inflection in its margin profile and profitability could lead the company to actually grow into this insane valuation in the coming years. With that in mind, I'll be upgrading Palantir from Sell to Buy in this article. The market has clearly demonstrated its willingness to pay a premium multiple for this stock, therefore I cannot continue recommending selling on a valuation basis alone. Despite clear valuation risks, I expect Palantir to continue to generate market-beating returns.

Tariffs and AIP Profitability

Trump's tariff gambit benefits Palantir in two primary ways. First, there's an important clause in the reciprocal tariff policy that US-derived components used in a final product will not be subject to tariffs. This gives the company an abundant incentive to deeply understand their global supply chains and pinpoint the exact tariff treatment of their goods. Luckily for Palantir, it has software that is purpose-built for this type of use case. Increasing supply chain complexity and the need to more deeply understand supply chains is strictly beneficial for an enterprise software company that simplifies these data feeds. Even with the recent tariff pause, companies will be on high alert for understanding their global supply chain. In this scope, knowledge is power, and Palantir empowers organizations with the full context of their operations.

Second, a higher-for-longer tariff environment incentivizes domestic manufacturing investment. This investment will be disproportionately directed toward robotics, AI, and automation so that goods can be made in America without rampant inflation. While I consider the investment in robotics machinery as a second-order effect of tariffs, I believe that investment in the software used to manage these devices is a third-order effect. Palantir, again, is in a prime position to pounce on this opportunity.

A defining feature of an exceptional business is that its products are resistant to this exact type of turmoil and uncertainty. I see the risks of a recessionary environment and geopolitical tensions as immaterial. Additionally, the process of reshored manufacturing operations increases demand for AIP.

Geopolitical uncertainty increases demand for Palantir software, supply chain complexity increases demand for Palantir software, factory automation increases demand for Palantir software... do you see the trend? The company is enjoying an outright explosion in demand during a historic AI driven bull run. Despite short term tariff-related volatility, the stock has held up exceptionally well even considering the clear valuation risks.

Additionally, I see little downside risk related to the recent defense department announcement of budget cuts. There is an enormous amount of wasteful spend in the defense department, but Palantir's software is mission-critical and drives efficiency. There would be no reason to cut spending on this software. In fact, it could lead to larger contracts over time as the defense department seeks to better understand its disparate operations and decrease total spend.

Investor Takeaway

My initial article on Palantir was a Buy at $16.57 where I stated there's a lot to love here. That sentiment remains, there is a lot to love and very little to dislike about Palantir fundamentally. The company is experiencing an explosion in demand and met this with an innovative go-to-market "bootcamp" strategy for customer onboarding. Annualized contract and remaining deal values are both booming, leading to an explosion in profitability and consistent expansions in the company's margin profile. The stock is becoming a free cashflow machine, leaving abundant cash to reinvest in future initiatives. Palantir has met the AI wave with open arms and there's very few companies better positioned to ride the wave.

Overall, despite the extremely elevated valuation and clear risks presented therein, I do believe Palantir will continue generating market-beating returns in the short-run and is worth a spot in a risk-loving investors' portfolio. Therefore, I'm upgrading Palantir to a Buy.

data.title: Tariff Panic? Why I'm Buying More Microsoft While Others Sell
data.description:
Like other tech giants - Apple (AAPL) and Amazon.com (AMZN) - I've also owned Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) since 2014. Although its growth isn’t as explosive as some of its peers in the Magnificent 7, at today's prices, I can’t help but see this as a perfect entry point.

With all the other high-growth tech stocks, you might be wondering if Microsoft is still a buy. Spoiler: my analysis for the company is a “strong buy” due to its AI advancements and its predictable income that translates to solid overall financials.

Quick Overview

Microsoft is a household name, so I’ll keep this intro short. Microsoft Corporation is a global company with a diversified portfolio of products. The entire company operates through three segments - Productivity and Business Processes (Office, LinkedIn, Dynamics), Intelligent Cloud (Azure, server products, GitHub), and More Personal Computing (Windows, Surface, Xbox). Moreover, Microsoft has made successful moves in cloud computing and investments in artificial intelligence, positioning it as a tech leader in enterprise solutions.

Recent Financials

Microsoft's Q2 ’25 financials reported solid numbers across the board. The top-line revenue increased by 12% year-over-year to $69.63 billion, while its operating income grew 17% to $31.65 billion - a clear indication that Microsoft is making significant progress despite economic uncertainties.

The company’s bottom line also experienced a 10% increase to $24.10 billion, translating to a quarterly EPS of $3.23, up from $2.93 in the same year-ago quarter. Without a doubt, this growth is a result of Microsoft’s positive momentum.

The same can be said if you look at the company’s segment revenues:

Its Intelligent Cloud segment reported 19% year-over-year growth, since the company’s investments in this segment are paying off as more businesses embrace “moving to the cloud”. Also, this means Microsoft’s Azure platform is keeping pace with its top competitors like AWS and Google Cloud.

The Productivity and Business Processes segment isn't doing too shabby either - it reported solid 14% growth on the backs of Microsoft 365 subscriptions and LinkedIn.

I’ve been a fan of subscription income since high school and wrote about it in my first book, The Financially Independent Millennial. I don’t know any better way to generate consistent earnings than hitting customers’ credit cards on a monthly basis. But I digress.

Microsoft’s More Personal Computing segment, however, came out with flat 0% growth, due to the 21% increase in Search and news advertising revenue, excluding traffic acquisition costs. This doesn’t surprise me as I can’t recently recall anyone buying or using a “Microsoft Surface” recently, except maybe on TV.

Despite that, Microsoft managed to boost its overall revenue by 12% Y.O.Y. I think this only means the company is successfully shifting from its old-school Windows business to where the real money is: Cloud services, subscriptions, and of course, AI.

5-YR MSFT Stock Price Performance

Over the past 5 years, Microsoft has an impressive 114.72% return with a remarkably consistent growth trajectory, which outpaces major market indices.

Although there have been “across the board” pullbacks thanks to U.S. President Donald Trump’s tariffs mandate, MSFT stock hit a 52-week high of $468.35 while establishing support above its 52-week low of $344.72. The pull-back is primarily driven by fear, short seller, and profit-taking events. I, too, have considered selling my MSFT stock, but, why?

From everything I’ve read, my conclusion is that tariffs can’t last forever - at least without setting off a disaster amounting to that of the Great Depression.

So yes, right now, while there’s lots of panic, I think we’re now starting to see a buying opportunity that we haven’t seen in years.

5-YR EPS Magnificent 7 Comparison

Looking at the graph, Microsoft’s EPS (orange line) has been on a steady growth trajectory over the past five years - the company has long established its position within the tech industry, so the stability makes sense.

We can see that META is the absolute leader when it comes to EPS figures. Actually, I pulled the trigger and initiated a position on the stock at the beginning of April, following my analysis on March 19, 2025.

That said, it doesn’t mean META is more of a buy.

Microsoft's consistently increasing EPS is also not something we should be overlooking. I mean, to me, the company operates with a figurative license to print money.

But for a clearer comparison, I’ve extracted the Magnificent 7’s 5-YR diluted EPS data.
